Party_Details.Name: Chong Hon Kuan Ivan

Facts.Text: Prior to 1996-12-20, Chong Hon Kuan Ivan was one of the three Original Shareholders of Eureka Advertising Pte Ltd, later known as Publicis Eureka Pte Ltd, together with Chang Hong Kaye Jimmy and Neo Kee Choon Thomas.

With effect from 1997-01-01, Chong Hon Kuan Ivan entered into an employment agreement with Publicis Eureka Pte Ltd under which he was employed as managing director and chief executive officer for five years, with a provision for renewal for another five years from 2002-01-01 if specified conditions regarding gross negligence or wilful misconduct were not triggered.
From 1997-01-01, Chong Hon Kuan Ivan held the positions of managing director and chief executive officer of Publicis Eureka Pte Ltd.

A board of directors meeting of Publicis Eureka Pte Ltd was scheduled for 2002-02-08 to consider, among other items, the conduct of Ivan Chong as managing director and chief executive officer as referred to in a letter dated 2002-11-06 from Publicis Worldwide to Ivan Chong.

On 2002-02-09, an adjourned board of directors meeting of Publicis Eureka Pte Ltd was held at which a resolution concerning the termination of Ivan Chong’s employment was proposed and put to a vote; Chong and Chang Hong Kaye Jimmy voted against the resolution and Neo Kee Choon Thomas abstained.
On 2002-02-09, a letter was sent to Ivan Chong on the letterhead of Publicis Eureka Pte Ltd confirming that his employment with the company was treated as terminated.
By 2002-01-01, disputes had arisen between Ivan Chong and other stakeholders of Publicis Eureka Pte Ltd concerning his employment and related agreements.
By 2002-01-01, Ivan Chong had commenced a derivative action under section 216A of the Companies Act in relation to matters involving Publicis Eureka Pte Ltd.

On 2002-02-08 or 2002-02-09, prior to the adjourned board meeting, Jean-Paul Morin informed Ivan Chong that Publicis Worldwide BV had a new settlement proposal and that if Chong did not accept this offer, a resolution concerning his dismissal would be put at the adjourned board meeting on 2002-02-09.

On 2002-02-08 or 2002-02-09, a new settlement proposal from Publicis Worldwide BV was communicated to Ivan Chong, which contained terms less favourable to him than those in an earlier settlement agreement between him and Publicis Worldwide BV and Publicis Groupe SA.
On 2002-02-08 or 2002-02-09, Ivan Chong did not accept the new settlement proposal communicated to him by Jean-Paul Morin.

On 2002-02-09, at the adjourned board meeting of Publicis Eureka Pte Ltd, a resolution concerning the termination of Ivan Chong’s employment was passed by a 3-2 majority, with three votes in favour, two votes against by Ivan Chong and Chang Hong Kaye Jimmy, and an abstention by Neo Kee Choon Thomas.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether individual directors of a company can be held personally liable in tort for conspiracy or for inducing a breach of contract when their collective vote at a board meeting results in the termination of a fellow director's employment agreement.
Party_Details.Rule: Under the common law principle established in *Said v Butt*, a company director acting bona fide and within the scope of their authority is treated in law as the alter ego of the company. Therefore, the director cannot be held personally liable in tort for inducing the company to breach a contract with a third party (or a fellow employee). Pleadings alleging conspiracy or inducement must show specific, independent acts outside the scope of the director's official corporate functions; a general assertion of an intention to injure is insufficient to strip away this corporate protection.
Party_Details.Application: Following a corporate restructuring, a subsidiary's board voted to terminate the CEO's employment contract. The dispute involves whether the overarching corporate hierarchy and the parent company's involvement provide grounds to strip individual directors of their standard immunity against personal tort claims.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court held that the individual directors could not be sued personally for conspiracy or inducing a breach of contract based on their votes at a board meeting. The court strongly affirmed the *Said v Butt* principle, emphasizing that a company acts through its directors. If directors act within the scope of their authority to terminate an employment contract, their acts are the acts of the company itself. The court rejected the plaintiff's argument that pre-meeting discussions among the directors constituted an independent, actionable conspiracy, noting that corporate decision-making naturally involves informal discussions outside the boardroom. Furthermore, the court ruled that simply pleading that the directors acted with 'malice' or an 'intention to injure' is insufficient to override the *Said v Butt* protection. Because the plaintiff's statement of claim failed to allege specific fraudulent or extra-corporate acts by the directors, the conspiracy and inducement claims were struck out.

Party_Details.Name: Levy Maurice

Facts.Text: From 15 January 1997 to 9 February 2002, Maurice Levy was a director of Publicis Singapore.
At all material times, Maurice Levy was the chairman and chief executive officer of Publicis France.
On 9 February 2002, Morin proposed a resolution to terminate Chong's employment with Publicis Singapore and, exercising his vote and the votes of Levy and Salto, voted in favour of the resolution.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether a company director, casting a proxy vote through a fellow director to terminate an executive's employment, can be held personally liable in tort for conspiring to induce the company to breach that employment contract.
Party_Details.Rule: A director acting within the scope of their corporate authority and bona fide in the company's affairs is protected by the *Said v Butt* principle. They are treated as acting as the company itself and cannot be personally liable for inducing the company to breach a contract. To sustain a claim against a director personally, a plaintiff must plead specific facts demonstrating that the director acted outside their official capacity or committed distinct, independent torts (such as fraud); generalized pleadings of 'malice' or 'conspiracy' surrounding a board vote are legally insufficient.
Party_Details.Application: Maurice Levy served as a director of Publicis Singapore and was the chairman of its parent company. During an adjourned board meeting concerning the continued employment of the Managing Director, Ivan Chong, Levy's vote was exercised by proxy (via Morin) in favor of terminating Chong's contract. Chong subsequently sued Levy personally, alleging that the pre-meeting discussions and the resulting proxy vote constituted an unlawful conspiracy to induce the company to breach his employment agreement.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court granted Levy's application to strike out the tort claims against him. The judge ruled that Levy's actions—discussing corporate matters and casting a proxy vote at a board meeting—fell squarely within the scope of his authority as a director. The court rejected the plaintiff's artificial distinction that directors could be sued for conspiracy if they agreed on a course of action *before* the formal board meeting, noting that corporate governance relies on continuous informal communication. Applying the *Said v Butt* principle, the court held that because Levy was acting as an organ of the company, he could not logically conspire with the company to induce it to breach its own contract. Since the plaintiff's pleadings offered only bare assertions of malice without detailing acts outside the scope of Levy's directorship, the claims were legally unsustainable and struck out.

Party_Details.Name: Salomon Salto

Facts.Text: At all material times, Salomon Salto was Senior Vice-President of Publicis Netherlands.
At all material times, Salomon Salto was a director of Publicis Eureka Pte Ltd.
Salomon Salto was one of the directors of Publicis Singapore in relation to the events leading to the termination of Ivan Chong’s employment.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether a corporate director can be subjected to personal liability for conspiracy or inducing a breach of contract merely for aligning with a majority vote to terminate a fellow executive's employment.
Party_Details.Rule: The doctrine established in *Said v Butt* shields corporate directors from personal tort liability for inducing a breach of contract when their actions are performed bona fide and within their official capacity on behalf of the company. A plaintiff cannot circumvent this protection merely by labeling the directors' collective decision-making process as a 'conspiracy'.
Party_Details.Application: Salomon Salto, acting as a director of Publicis Eureka Pte Ltd, participated (via proxy) in the majority board decision that resulted in the termination of Ivan Chong's employment as Managing Director. The plaintiff alleges that Salto’s participation in the pre-meeting consensus and the ultimate vote constituted an actionable tort of conspiracy to induce a contractual breach.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: Consistent with its ruling on the co-defendants, the court found that Salto's involvement in the termination process—specifically, participating in a board vote—was an act performed in his capacity as a director. The court firmly rejected the argument that pre-meeting discussions among directors strip them of their corporate protection. The statement of claim failed to provide specific, independent facts indicating that Salto acted fraudulently or completely outside his corporate mandate. Therefore, under the *Said v Butt* rule, Salto's actions were attributed to the company, rendering the personal tort claims against him legally defective and subject to being struck out.

Party_Details.Name: Jean-Paul Morin

Facts.Text: At all material times, Jean-Paul Morin was the chairman and chief executive officer of Publicis Worldwide BV.
At all material times, Jean-Paul Morin was the chief financial officer and/or corporate secretary of Publicis Groupe SA.
At all material times, Jean-Paul Morin was a director of Publicis Eureka Pte Ltd.

On 4 February 2002, Jean-Paul Morin called for a meeting of the board of directors of Publicis Eureka Pte Ltd to be held on 8 February 2002 to consider, among other items, the conduct of Ivan Chong as managing director and chief executive officer as set out in a letter of 6 November 2002 from Publicis Worldwide to Ivan Chong.

On 9 February 2002, at the adjourned board meeting of Publicis Eureka Pte Ltd, Jean-Paul Morin proposed a resolution to terminate Ivan Chong’s employment and exercised his vote and the votes of Maurice Levy and Salomon Salto in favor of the resolution.

On or before 9 February 2002, Jean-Paul Morin informed Ivan Chong that Publicis Netherlands had a new settlement proposal and that if Chong did not accept this new offer, he would be dismissed at the adjourned Board of Directors Meeting on 9 February 2002.
On 9 February 2002, Jean-Paul Morin, purportedly for and on behalf of Publicis Eureka Pte Ltd, sent a letter to Ivan Chong confirming the termination of Chong’s employment.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether a director who actively coordinates a board meeting, conveys a corporate settlement ultimatum, and formally executes the termination of a fellow executive can be held personally liable in tort for conspiring to breach the executive's employment contract.
Party_Details.Rule: Corporate officers performing their designated functions—such as convening board meetings, negotiating settlements on behalf of the company, and executing board resolutions—are acting as the company's alter ego. Under the rule in *Said v Butt*, they cannot be held personally liable for inducing the company to breach a contract unless the plaintiff specifically pleads and proves that the officer acted completely outside their corporate authority or engaged in distinct, independent tortious conduct (e.g., fraud).
Party_Details.Application: Jean-Paul Morin acted as the primary conduit between the parent company and the local subsidiary, Publicis Eureka Pte Ltd. Morin convened the crucial board meeting, presented a revised settlement ultimatum to Ivan Chong, cast his own vote alongside proxy votes to terminate Chong, and officially signed the termination letter. The plaintiff argues that Morin's proactive coordination of the dismissal before and during the board meeting constituted a malicious, actionable conspiracy to procure a breach of his employment contract, rendering Morin personally liable.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court assessed Morin's actions—convening meetings, negotiating settlements, voting, and signing termination documents—and concluded they were quintessential duties of a corporate director. The court systematically dismantled the plaintiff's argument that coordinating an executive's dismissal constituted an external, actionable conspiracy. Applying the *Said v Butt* principle, the judge reiterated that a director acting within their scope of authority is legally indivisible from the company regarding corporate contracts. Because the pleadings failed to allege that Morin committed any independent tort or acted outside his corporate mandate—relying instead on inadequate, generic claims of malice—the personal tort claims against him were deemed legally unsustainable and struck out.

Party_Details.Name: Publicis Eureka Pte Ltd

Facts.Text: Publicis Eureka Pte Ltd, previously known as Eureka Advertising Pte Ltd, is a company incorporated in Singapore.

As part of the acquisition on or about 1996-12-20, Publicis Eureka Pte Ltd entered into employment agreements with each of the Original Shareholders, including an agreement with Ivan Chong for a five-year term commencing 1997-01-01 with a renewal provision for another five years from 2002-01-01 specified conditions.

On 2002-02-04, a notice was issued for a board of directors meeting of Publicis Eureka Pte Ltd to be held on 2002-02-08 to consider, among other items, the conduct of Ivan Chong as managing director and chief executive officer as set out in a letter dated 2002-11-06 from Publicis Worldwide to Ivan Chong.

On 2002-02-08, a board of directors meeting of Publicis Eureka Pte Ltd was held at which no substantive discussion took place regarding Ivan Chong’s conduct as managing director and chief executive officer.

On 2002-02-09, an adjourned board of directors meeting of Publicis Eureka Pte Ltd was held at which a resolution concerning the termination of Ivan Chong’s employment was proposed and passed by a 3-2 majority, with three votes in favor, two votes against by Ivan Chong and Chang Hong Kaye Jimmy, and an abstention by Neo Kee Choon Thomas.
On 2002-02-09, a letter was issued in the name of Publicis Eureka Pte Ltd to Ivan Chong confirming that his employment with the company was treated as terminated.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether the corporate entity, having terminated an executive's employment via a majority board resolution, provides a basis for the executive to sue the individual directors personally in tort for the company's contractual breach.
Party_Details.Rule: A corporation acts through its board of directors. When a company breaches a contract based on a board resolution, the primary cause of action lies in contract against the company itself. The individual directors who passed the resolution cannot be sued personally in tort for inducing that breach, provided they were acting within the scope of their authority as the directing mind and will of the corporate entity.
Party_Details.Application: Publicis Eureka Pte Ltd held a binding employment contract with Ivan Chong. Following disputes regarding his conduct, the company convened a board meeting where a majority of the directors voted to terminate Chong's contract. The company immediately issued a formal termination letter. The plaintiff subsequently attempted to hold the individual directors personally liable in tort for the company's decision to terminate him.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court analyzed the termination strictly as a corporate act executed by Publicis Eureka Pte Ltd. The judge reiterated that because a company is an artificial entity, it relies entirely on its directors to make and execute decisions, such as terminating an employment contract. The court clarified that while the company itself might be liable for breach of contract (an issue not resolved in this specific striking-out application), the individual directors who effectuated that corporate act are shielded from personal tort liability under the *Said v Butt* principle. The judgment thus reinforced the protective boundary between corporate contractual liability and personal tort liability.

Party_Details.Name: Publicis Worldwide B.V.

Facts.Text: Publicis Worldwide BV is a company incorporated in the Netherlands.

Following a corporate restructuring after 1996-12-20, Publicis Worldwide BV became the holder of the 60% shareholding in Publicis Eureka Pte Ltd that had previously been acquired by Publicis Groupe SA from the Original Shareholders.

On 2002-02-08 or 2002-02-09, Publicis Worldwide BV put forward a new settlement proposal to Ivan Chong through Jean-Paul Morin, containing terms less favourable than those in an earlier settlement agreement involving Publicis Worldwide BV and Publicis Groupe SA.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether a majority shareholder's presentation of a revised, less favorable settlement proposal prior to an executive's termination exposes the subsidiary's directors to personal tort liability for conspiracy.
Party_Details.Rule: The presentation of a settlement proposal by a parent company or majority shareholder during an employment dispute represents a corporate negotiation tactic. It does not independently transform the subsequent board actions of the subsidiary's directors into an actionable, extra-corporate conspiracy that strips them of their immunity under the *Said v Butt* principle.
Party_Details.Application: Publicis Worldwide BV held a 60% majority stake in Publicis Eureka Pte Ltd. During the escalating dispute with Ivan Chong, Publicis Worldwide BV formulated a new, less favorable settlement proposal, which was presented to Chong just before the crucial board meeting. When Chong rejected this proposal, the subsidiary's board proceeded to terminate him. The plaintiff attempts to use the parent company's settlement ultimatum as evidence of a malicious, coordinated conspiracy orchestrated by the individual directors.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court viewed Publicis Worldwide BV's involvement—specifically its presentation of a settlement proposal—as part of the standard factual matrix of corporate dispute resolution. The parent company's actions provided context for why the termination vote occurred but did not alter the fundamental legal dynamic: the directors of the subsidiary were acting within their official capacities when they executed the termination. Consequently, the parent company's involvement did not furnish the 'something more' (such as fraud) required to pierce the corporate veil and impose personal tort liability on the individual directors.

Party_Details.Name: Publicis Groupe SA

Facts.Text: Publicis Groupe SA is a public listed company incorporated in France.
Following a corporate restructuring after 1996-12-20, the 60% shareholding in Publicis Eureka Pte Ltd held by Publicis Groupe SA was transferred to Publicis Worldwide BV.

Party_Details.Issue: How the ultimate parent company's original acquisition of the subsidiary establishes the framework for evaluating subsequent employment disputes and claims of director conspiracy.
Party_Details.Rule: The historical acquisition structure and corporate hierarchy provide necessary context for understanding employment agreements but do not bypass the established rule that individual directors of a subsidiary, when acting within their scope of authority to terminate an employee, are immune from personal tort liability for inducing a breach of contract.
Party_Details.Application: Publicis Groupe SA originally acquired a 60% stake in Eureka Advertising, subsequently restructuring the entity and transferring the stake to Publicis Worldwide BV. As part of this initial acquisition, Ivan Chong's employment contract was drafted and executed. The legal tension is whether this overarching corporate architecture implicates the individual subsidiary directors in a broader tortious conspiracy when they later voted to terminate that specific employment contract.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court utilized the facts concerning Publicis Groupe SA purely to establish the historical origins of Ivan Chong's employment contract and the corporate chain of command. The parent company's original involvement in structuring the business did not affect the court's core legal analysis regarding the striking-out application. The court maintained focus on the actions of the individual directors at the time of termination, concluding that the overarching corporate structure did not provide grounds to strip the directors of their immunity under the *Said v Butt* principle.
